content: Photosynthesis serves as the primary bioenergetic process enabling plants to convert solar energy into chemical energy, fundamentally sustaining their metabolic and developmental demands. Through the light-dependent reactions, photophosphorylation generates ATP and NADPH, which fuel the Calvin–Benson cycle’s fixation of atmospheric CO₂ into triose phosphates. These reduced carbon skeletons underpin a vast array of biosynthetic pathways, supplying substrates for cellulose synthesis, secondary metabolite production, and respiratory metabolism. Beyond mere carbohydrate provision, photosynthesis modulates redox homeostasis and generates signaling molecules that influence stomatal behavior, gene expression, and resource allocation. The oxygenic nature of the light reactions also supports cellular respiration and contributes to atmospheric oxygen levels, indirectly impacting plant and ecosystem physiology. Collectively, photosynthesis integrates energy capture with carbon assimilation, orchestrating plant growth, development, and adaptive responses to fluctuating environmental conditions. This central role underscores its influence not only on individual plant fitness but also on global carbon cycling and biospheric productivity.
